sentences.text: My name is Charlotte Kahane.
K-A-H-A-N-E.
My name at, at birth?
My maiden name.
It's Feiner, F-E-I-N-E-R.
No.
No.
It's the 30th of October, 1926.
My age today is 70.
I was born in Lwow-- spelled L-W-O-W-- Poland.
That's east-south Poland.
Lwow was a very beautiful city.
It had Austrian influence.
And in-- like, my father-- in my father's time was in the Austrian army, because it used to belong to Kaiser Joseph-- Franz Joseph-- regime.
Of course, it wasn't in our time, because it became Polish in 1918.
And it was-- it maintained that sovereignty until 1939.
In 1939, as you know, Poland was partitioned.
And that part of it in which we lived, that region, belonged to Russia.
It was partitioned between the Germans and the Russians.
And the Russians stayed in our town [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from 1939 to June 1941, which marks the-- marked the start of the Second World War between Germany and Russia.
My town in 1939 had a population of 360,000 people, and from-- the, the, the Jewish population stood at 150,000 at the time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Though I was not in Lwow during the German occupation, but it's perhaps appropriate, because I was born and raised there, to tell you a little bit about it.
The, the-- there was a very low rate of survival in Lwow.
I was told that when the Russians came back to Lwow after the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they found about 1,200 Jewish survivors, though only 400 of them were in the real ghetto of, of Lwow.
They came out of hiding.
I know that most of the-- they transported about 15,000 Jews.
The Germans, on entering Lwow, they transported-- after entering Lwow, they transported about 15,000 Jews to Bergen-Belsen, who were never heard of after.
And they, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they arranged a ghetto in special parts of town and also working camps.
And I was not in Lwow at the time.
And I need to expand on this to explain my case.
I grew up as a child in Lwow.
I had two brothers-- one was-- his name was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is where I become emotional.
I'm sorry.
[CRYING] His name was Moses Lieb.
And he was 14 years older than I.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I had a younger brother who was only 18 months my junior.
My older brother studied in Paris, being so much older than I.
And we were-- he was a little bit detached from the family because he spent most of the time in France.
Unfortunately, at the start of the war, he came home.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My father lived with us in Lwow.
I want to explain this.
My parents were divorced when I was six.
And this is where I get back to where I was during the war.
I'll get back to you.
I went to a-- my friends were mostly Jewish, though I had some-- I did have some Gentile friends.
And I went to a Catholic school because Catholic schools were public schools in Lwow.
And strange enough, it was one of the regions in Poland where you could say there was a class ghetto.
And I'm talking as a girl of under 14, who from her seventh-- from her time of enrollment-- the enrollment at the primary school in Poland did not start until seven, so-- the age of seven.
And there was-- the Jewish girls always sat separately, were divided by benches from the Catholic girls.
And we had to get up to the Catholic prayer every morning, of course.
We knew it by heart, which [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the-- from what flows from the interview later on stood me by good stead, in good stead because it identified me as a Catholic.
Because there's only one word for it-- anti-Semitism.
It was prevalent at schools, though perhaps it didn't go-- it wasn't an order [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from the top.
This was prevalent in higher institutions because there was numerous classes in Lwow where students were not-- there was a very limited number of Jewish students permitted to study.
And that explains my brother's absence from Poland during his years of study, that he had to go to France.
But it was just quite natural for us to accept it, that we were divided by several benches from the Catholic girls.
Though I must say that at primary school and then at medium school-- because I went to school there until the age of 13-- there was no hostility amongst us.
But there were some schools, I believe, that there was.
Outside the school?
I didn't experience it personally, but I had-- I could observe it everywhere.
I-- for example, there was a Jewish shop not far from us.
And it was a butcher shop.
And at that time-- in my times, butcher shops were divided, to an extent, and they were selling also-- they were also selling fish.
The-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the Jewish consumers of fish were mostly Friday customers.
You know, they used to buy fish for Shabbat.
And on holidays-- during holidays or approaching holidays, such as Christmas and Easter, you would have academics and students, Poles, called Endecks, from the nearby higher institutions.
And it was customary to gang up before the shop and stop the customers from going into the kosher butcher.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because it wasn't frequented only by Jews.
I'm not sure whether-- how kosher this butcher was, but I had no perception, you know, of, of being more kosher than-- or less kosher.
I knew we were Jewish, and we're using that meat.
And this store was frequented by non-Jews also.
My family was more traditional, though I-- and this came-- perhaps my father was religious, but this came because the family split up.
And we had hired help to run the household.
So I would say that it wasn't a kosher household, but, but yes, we were observant in the sense that there was a lot of commotion back home, for example, at Pesach.
You know, they used to get the dishes and-- for Pesach, they were specially packed in special boxes.
They had to get them down from the attics, kind of, to use them.
And, and back they went.
And that was quite a big thing.
And, and you say whether I ever experienced anti-Semitism-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not in a way, you know, where you were engaged in discussions as a little girl with, with people.
But my grandparents lived [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] about an hour and a quarter-- about, yes-- from Lwow in a small shtetl.
And it was quite picturesque.
The, the station was quite picturesque.
And it had flowers.
And, and I got off the station, off the train, to go to my grandparents.
And there were some Polish ladies there.
And of course, I was a city girl, which was different and differently dressed to the peasants and the locals.
I wore plaits, and one remarked that how cute that girl looks.
And the other sort of nudged her.
She's Jewish.
And they all turned away.
This is the kind of experience that I had as a child in Poland.
How could I take it?
It was, it was natural.
But I was, I was liberal, in a way-- not in the sense that I approved of it.
But for example, my grandmother had a neighbor not very far away.
And she was a schoolteacher.
Her name was Miss [NON-ENGLISH].
And Miss [NON-ENGLISH] was fond of me.
So one-- it was some kind of, of a Polish holiday, of a Catholic holiday, and she brought me a platter of biscuits.
And my grandmother was very kosher.
She wouldn't want that platter of biscuits to have at home.
And she kept threatening me that I will die unless I give the, the biscuits-- because they weren't kosher-- unless I give them to the dog.
And I thought to myself, I'm going to try whether God's, God's going to punish me for eating those biscuits.
And I never gave in.
Even then, I was stubborn.
What I can tell you about my home.
My father was-- when I opened my eyes, he was always praying in the morning-- [CRYING] I'm sorry-- [CRYING] before he went to business.
But he was a very good man, and I think fairly broken, because mother left us.
And he didn't have much time for us.
We didn't want anything or need any material comforts for the standard that were maintained for children in Poland.
And-- but it would hurt me, say, that for my birthday my classmates would come, and my mother wasn't there.
[CRYING] Now it's different.
Divorce rates are high, and kids are accustomed to it.
But at that time, it was quite a stigma [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in, in society-- particularly in Jewish society.
What I can tell you.
I can tell you that I had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] wonderful cousins.
Some were older than I.
They used to take me for holidays.
And one, one of my cousins was, was my age.
I spent much time with them.
And I was just a normal, happy child.
We-- my father owned a, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a fur store.
And it was mostly a store which was not fashionable ladies' furs, but mostly-- there were some from what I could observe.
No one sort of consulted me whether it was-- I wasn't fashion-minded at that time.
But it was mostly for people who came from the country to shop to have some kind of protection for the winter, because the winters were very severe.
And the store was doing very well.
He employed several people.
That's what I know.
And my greatest treat was on Saturday night.
My dad used to take me out to a restaurant.
And that wasn't an acceptable mode of, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] of behavior, of attitude, particularly children.
And I thought that was my highlight, usually, of the week.
I was-- when I said I didn't want anything, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't short of anything.
I had in winter a season ticket to go ice skating.
And I went to rhythmical gym.
And that's about all I sort of, I can tell you.
Only I don't know whether you want me to continue about Lwow.
My mother-- I'm coming to this.
I usually spent my annual holidays in Luck.
Spelled-- it was spelled like you would spell English, in English, Luck, L-U-C-K, though I have seen it in an English translation spelled L-U-T-S-K.
And she remarried.
She married a lawyer, called Dr.
Lampert.
And I usually spent my school holidays with my mother.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In that year, 1941, it was-- June is the warm season in Poland.
The war started on the 22nd of September, 1941.
And about five days or six days before, I went to Mother's.
They expected me at the station.
Everything was normal.
And just my father and my brother, my younger brother, brought me to the railway station.
But when-- just five, five or six days on-- after I arrived, the Germans invaded Luck.
The town fell to the Germans.
And I was trapped there.
I couldn't go back to my brothers and father.
I was left there with my mother.
And the bad luck was that just when the Germans came in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took hostages from the ranks of the intelligentsia.
And my mother's husband-- I won't say stepfather, because it sounds strange while your father was alive to have a stepfather as well.
They took him away.
And Mother and I were just on our own at the time.
When the Germans came into town, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they initially [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] called up sort of some-- about 3,000-- Luck had a population of 20,000 Jews in 1939.
And that included some Jews who were refugees from the western part of Poland where the Germans landed two years earlier.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So they recruited-- the Nazis recruited a lot of these men, allegedly, for work, but they've never-- about 3,000 of them-- and they never came back.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Whereas [NON-ENGLISH] Lampert-- that was my mother's husband.
I was very fond of him.
I called him by the name, and not like-- I didn't call Father.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When they arrested him, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we tried, Mother and I, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to, to get him out.
I don't know much.
I was only 14 at the time.
And I don't know much about the bureaucratic setup.
But we went to the Judenrat, my mother and I.
And I remember the chairman of the Judenrat was a Jewish lawyer, called Pincus.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my mother wanted to invest in her last piece of jewelry to bribe someone close to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] administration to get him out.
And I remember that he used to slip into the hands of Poles he was arrested little notes.
One said, my life hangs on a thin thread.
That's what I remember, but I've never seen him again.
When the Germans came to Luck in December of the year that they arrived, 1941-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they arrived in June.
And what I'm telling you about the arrest of the young men was in July.
Within that break from July to December, they managed to organize a ghetto.
They pushed all the Jews into the ghetto.
And that was December 1941.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were terrible conditions in the ghetto, but [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you don't realize how inventive you can be, even when you're young.

And I spoke Polish without an accent-- and I think a lot of Jewish people exposed themselves, particularly those whose language was not Polish at home-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I could get out of the ghetto and to occasionally arrange some food for Mother and I.
Yes, go on.
Well, it's very hard to measure distances from the perception of a 14-year-old in Poland with our distances in Australia.
I've spent most of my life here.
But I would say it was like going from another suburb, from one suburb to another.
That was the ghetto.
And--
No.
We had lost all contact.
I don't know whether he was trapped-- he and my brothers were trapped at the very beginning.
I don't know that.
I heard different versions from people, some from Lwow, whom I met after the war.
I'm sort of getting away from the chronology, but I have to tell you this, that someone told me.
Lwow had one of its biggest working camps.
It was called Janowska, spelled with a J.
Janowska-- J-A-N-O-W-S-K-A.
That was one version of it.
Another version of it was-- and that is a fact that this happened, whether any of my male members of the family were there, I will never know for sure.
They hounded all the Jews into the Jewish-- it was called the Temple.
That was probably a reform-- more of a reform synagogue and the largest one in Melbourne-- a very prominent one.
And they, they burned it to the ground while the Jews were inside.
So that was the other version of how my peoples did not survive.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
The ghetto.

On the-- between 19th-- the 19th and 23rd or 25th-- I don't know the date exactly. 19-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in August 1942, between 19th, 19th and 25th, could be 26th, they gathered 17,000 Jews-- one, seven-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] at the place called Polonka on the outer periphery of Lutsk and shot them there.
They murdered them there.

It's very strange that I can talk about it in 1997, be alive, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I have seen it all, because they came to the, to our ghetto dwelling [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and they were- they collected all female members.
There were 13 of us-- amongst them, three children, two younger than myself.
I told you before that Dr.
Lampert was not there, because he was initially taken as part of the intelligentsia hostages.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sorry.
